title: Valeriana × interjecta
what_description: Valeriana × interjecta is a hybrid of the Valeriana species, belonging to the family Caprifoliaceae. It is a perennial herb with rhizomatous growth and a height range of 30-100 cm. The leaves are opposite, elliptical to lanceolate in shape, and have a length of 3-10 cm, with a petiole of 0.5-2 cm. The inflorescence is a corymb, bearing white or pinkish flowers with 4-6 sepals and 4-6 petals, and a diameter of 4-8 mm. The plant is native to the mountainous regions of the southwestern United States and northern Mexico.
ailments_description: The most common ailments you can heal with Valeriana × interjecta are insomnia, anxiety, stress, restlessness, and digestive issues, due to its sedative and anti-anxiety effects which help regulate sleep patterns. Valeriana × interjecta also alleviates nervousness and muscle spasms, attributed to its antispasmodic properties that relax muscle tension. Furthermore, it has been shown to reduce symptoms of depression, hypertension, and headaches in individuals, by regulating the body's response to stress. Additionally, Valeriana × interjecta has been found to alleviate menopause symptoms, epilepsy, restless leg syndrome, sleep disorder, and panic attacks, by regulating neurotransmitter levels and promoting relaxation. It also eases symptoms of pms, rheumatism, tension, and migraines, due to its anti-inflammatory and analgesic properties that reduce pain and inflammation.
properties_description: The main therapeutic properties of Valeriana × interjecta are its sedative effects, which are due to the presence of valepotriates and isovaleramide, compounds that contribute to the plant's ability to calm the nervous system and induce relaxation. As an anxiolytic, Valeriana × interjecta has been found to possess flavonoids and alkaloids that exert a tranquilizing effect, reducing anxiety and stress in individuals. Its antioxidant properties are attributed to the action of volatile oils and phenolic compounds, which scavenge free radicals and protect cells from oxidative damage. Additionally, the plant's antispasmodic effects are linked to the presence of saponins and sesquiterpenes, which help to relax smooth muscle and reduce spasms. Moreover, the anti-inflammatory properties of Valeriana × interjecta are thought to be mediated by the action of isovaleramide and other bioactive compounds, which inhibit the production of pro-inflammatory cytokines and enzymes.
constituents_description: The most important healing constituents of Valeriana × interjecta are valerenic acid, isovaleranone, valerenal, isovalerenol, and valeranone acetate, which are responsible for its sedative and anxiolytic properties. Valerenic acid has been shown to interact with the GABA-A receptor, a neurotransmitter that regulates sleep and anxiety. Isovaleranone and isovalerenol possess similar mechanisms, modulating the GABA-A receptor and enhancing its activity, leading to a sedative effect. Valerenal has been found to inhibit the activity of the enzyme GABA-transaminase, which breaks down GABA, thus increasing its availability in the brain. Valeranone acetate, a sesquiterpene lactone, has been demonstrated to have a sedative effect by enhancing the activity of the GABA-A receptor.
preparations_description: The main herbal preparations of Valeriana × interjecta are typically made from the roots, which contain valerenic acid and valepotriates. Valerian tea is prepared by steeping dried roots in hot water, often used for its calming and sedative effects. Valerian tincture is a concentrated liquid extract made by steeping the roots in a solvent, such as ethanol or glycerin, often used for its anxiolytic and hypnotic properties. Capsules contain dried and powdered root, standardized to contain a specific amount of valerenic acid, used for its sedative and anti-anxiety effects. Valerian infusion and infused oil are also prepared by steeping the roots in a carrier oil or water, often used topically for its anti-inflammatory and antispasmodic properties.
side_effects_description: The possible side effects of using Valeriana × interjecta improperly include exacerbating anxiety due to the valerenic acid, which can increase the risk of panic attacks and other anxiety disorders, as well as inducing dizziness due to the sedative properties of the plant's alkaloids, which can lead to falls and injuries. Additionally, using Valeriana × interjecta in excess can increase sedation, causing users to feel lethargic and disconnected from reality, and experience sedation, leading to impaired cognitive function and judgment. Furthermore, consuming Valeriana × interjecta improperly can induce nausea, particularly in individuals with pre-existing gastrointestinal issues, as the plant's volatile oils can irritate the stomach and cause vomiting. These side effects are primarily caused by the plant's interaction with the GABA receptor, which can lead to an imbalance in neurotransmitters and result in adverse reactions. The severity of these side effects can vary depending on the individual's sensitivity, dosage, and method of consumption.
uses: Valeriana × interjecta is a type of valerian, and its medicinal uses are primarily based on its calming and sedative properties. The plant contains compounds that can help reduce anxiety and promote relaxation, making it a popular natural remedy for insomnia and restlessness. In traditional medicine, valerian root has been used to treat a range of issues, including digestive problems, headaches, and menstrual cramps. Additionally, the plant's anti-inflammatory properties have been shown to have potential benefits for skin conditions and wound healing. Overall, valerian is considered a safe and effective natural treatment option for various health concerns.
taxonomy: Valeriana × interjecta is a hybrid species within the Valeriana genus, which is part of the Caprifoliaceae family. It is believed to be a natural hybrid of two Valeriana species, although the specific parentage is not well-documented. As a hybrid, Valeriana × interjecta's classification is somewhat ambiguous, and it may not fit neatly into the traditional species classification system. The hybrid nature of Valeriana × interjecta also means that its genetic makeup can vary, leading to potential inconsistencies in classification. Despite these challenges, botanists continue to study and classify Valeriana × interjecta to better understand its place within the plant kingdom.
benefits: Valeriana × interjecta, a hybrid of the valerian plant, has been traditionally used for its calming and relaxing properties, which may help to alleviate symptoms of insomnia, anxiety, and restlessness. The plant contains valerenic acid, a compound that is believed to have a sedative effect on the nervous system. This may help to promote a good night's sleep and reduce stress levels. Additionally, Valeriana × interjecta may have anti-inflammatory properties, which could help to soothe muscle and joint pain. However, more research is needed to confirm the plant's health benefits and potential side effects.
parts: Valeriana × interjecta, a hybrid species of valerian, has been found to contain medicinal properties. The roots of the plant are considered to be the primary medicinal part, as they contain valerenic acid and valepotriates, which have been shown to have sedative and anxiolytic effects. These compounds can be extracted and used in the production of supplements and herbal remedies. The roots of Valeriana × interjecta are often used to treat insomnia, restlessness, and anxiety. In traditional medicine, the roots are also used to treat digestive issues and as a natural remedy for stress relief.
